speaker: interviewee
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Many times I wonder. You, you cannot understand it, you know, when you-- I suppose that nobody can, you know. Because again, how lucky, how fortunate. Sometimes I feel. [LAUGHS] I don't know. But it's silly to say that I'm alive. I mean, you know, even when I, I mean, I certainly wouldn't think commit suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. And you have those wonderful children and grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are OK. But, but it's, it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number six. This is tape number six, with Anna Fischer on November 1, 1994, in Los Angeles, California. I think that Anna Fischer has finished her story for us. And what we will do next is show, show some of the photographs that she was able to save from before the war and of her children and her grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, I have to show you the photograph.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll start the tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm ready, and I'm rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Anna, could you tell me who this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is my father passport picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name? His name was Alter Lipszyc. And do you know approximately when this photograph was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. Probably sometimes in the 20s or 30 years. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know where it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was taken in Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lodz, Lodz where? Lodz, Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lodz, Poland. [LAUGHTER] We lived [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's go to the next one. All right. Could you tell me who all of these people are from left-- from top to the top?

speaker: interviewee
text: From top was my sister Sarah, which we used to call [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Below her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Below her are my two older brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, wait, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to cut. OK I'm now on--

speaker: interviewer
text: The middle--

speaker: interviewer
text: --The two boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are these two boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the left is my brother Moishe. And to the right is my-- he's the oldest brother. Moishe is older than I am. And Rafael is the oldest brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture of your brothers, as well as the picture of your sister, where were these photographs taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, well, my sister, I suppose, probably was taken also maybe for the school, [LAUGHS] you know, in-- in Lodz, Poland. And the picture of the boys was taken when we were on vacation. This was also in Poland. I don't know, maybe it was [NON-ENGLISH] you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know approximately when these photographs were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I could guess that her picture that was taken-- she's still in school, '35, '36. I have no idea. And, and their pictures also. You know, they were, they were not, they were maybe 17, 19, or 18, 20. I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was in the late '30s?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You want to go on to the next one?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next one is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, wait, he's not ready. Who is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my younger brother. His name is Lieb. Libek, we used to call him. And he was-- he's probably here not more than about nine years old, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know approximately what year this photograph would have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably the same time, you know, in the late, in the '30s. [INTERPOSING VOICES] This was all made in the mid-- this is all in the '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, where was this photograph--

speaker: interviewee
text: These were all in, this all was where we lived, this Lodz, Lodz, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Why don't we go on to the next one? Can you tell me who this picture is of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of me, taken by a patient in Weinsberg, by [NON-ENGLISH] in the TB sanatorium, when I--

speaker: interviewer
text: In what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1944 in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is when you were still--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was when I was a non-Jewish person, [LAUGHS] pretending to be a non-Jewish person.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. All right, can you tell us about this photograph and who's in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was taken also in Weinsberg, in 1944. To the left was-- is a Jewish girl. Well, her name was Eva-- Eva [NON-ENGLISH], I think. But she had a different, different [LAUGHS] last name at that time. Because [NON-ENGLISH] was her real name. And the next one is I, in the center, Anna Wojcik. [LAUGHS] And the third one is a Polish girl, by the name Yanke-- I don't remember her last name-- who was the only true friend of ours. [LAUGHS] And she was the one, the only one that, after the delegation, went to remove us from the Polish barracks and said these persons came. She was the only one that protested. And she says, I am going to remain her friend. She was really a good friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, could you tell us exactly what your name-- what your Polish name was during-- the Polish name you used during--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my Polish name was Anna Wojcik, W-O-J-- [LAUGHS] I always stop between G and J-- [LAUGHTER] C-I-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it?

speaker: interviewer
text: For this one. Could you tell us about this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: After, after the liberation, when I was in Germany in Stuttgart, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's a displaced person-- actually, it was a Jewish displaced person camp, by then organized by [NON-ENGLISH]. I applied to the American military. It was the American military government, for some sort of a job. There was only one left. And I didn't know what to do with myself. Then I was appointed to become-- I mean, it is a fancy name, but it didn't amount to much-- special investigator for public health, for the public health section. And my job would-- was a field job. I was supposed to visit pharmacies and this dental depots, whose owners, Nazis, were supposed to have been removed from their places, from their offices and replaced by Americans-- what did you call-- in German, you call it [GERMAN]. American substitute, total American-- a German who was-- who was not a Nazi. And this was something which I was supposed to come in unexpectedly. And they should have-- they should, I should make sure that this person who is, who was a war criminal shouldn't be there. And they were supposed to show me their books, to show me that they are also the-- that they are complying with whatever rules and regulations there were. I did that for about a couple of months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this document was your ID, was your identification?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this document was my identification. I think I had one which also had a picture. But this one, I think, pertains to the, to the, to the depots, to the dental depots. But I don't know what I did with the other one. But this was an official document from the office of the military government in Stuttgart.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think I took the one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, can you tell us about this photograph, and when  it was taken and where? This photograph was taken in Los Angeles, in 1948, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the backyard of one of our Holocaust survivors, by the name [NON-ENGLISH], who is not alive anymore. And what we were doing is sending food packages to Germany, to the displaced persons. Because they were still kept in the displaced persons camps, and they did not have-- the, there was, they couldn't-- they couldn't come to the United States, because the law did not permit them. And we were feeling very strongly about it, that especially as a Jewish refugees, that don't have anywhere else to go, there should the law be changed. But there was, there was a quota system in the United States at the time. And only president, at the time of the President Truman, this was changed. And people were getting visas to come to the United States. So this is one of those photos of our, our group of the Holocaust survivors that was already organizing, in order to help the other survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell me where you and your husband are in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were both-- I am at the very right. And Benno is in the white shirt, next to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now I just want-- you can hear me on the microphone, can't you? I just wanted to say that the photographs of Anna Fischer's children and grandchildren can now-- will be found at the end of the-- at the end of her husband's tape, which we will do next.
